As the table above shows, AMLP’s portfolio is characterized by high dividend yields, medium to low multiples, low growth and high profitability. The fund is cheaper than the S&P 500, which trades at 29.5 times earnings, but is growing less quickly than that index. AMLP's dividend yield is far above average, which is likely the main reason investors on Seeking Alpha are interested in it. On the whole, AMLP’s holdings look sensible, perhaps even a little undervalued.

Future Potential

Many investors are uncomfortable with oil and gas stocks, because petroleum-based fuels are seen as carrying obsolescence risk. Every year, renewable energy grows faster than oil, pointing to the possibility of a future when electricity from the grid replaces gasoline. In this author’s opinion, the fear is misguided: oil has many uses apart from just energy, including chemicals and pavement. I wrote in a recent article about the Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF, (XLE) oil prices are likely to remain reasonably high going forward, high enough for most big oil companies to be profitable, though probably not enough to propel lightning-fast growth.

AMLP is mainly an oil fund, so oil prices are naturally a factor in how well it will perform going forward. However, it is primarily involved in pipelines/midstream rather than exploration and production. The midstream sector makes up about 50% of AMLP's portfolio. This fact means that AMLP does not excessively depend on high oil prices to make money. Pipelines charge mostly fixed, long-term fees on 10-20 year contracts; occasionally their contracts have oil price-based incentive fees, but such fees are not essential to their profitability. The pipeline business model is more similar to real estate than oil exploration.

Considering the above, I think that AMLP’s portfolio stocks have the potential to grow modestly over time, leading to corresponding growth in their dividend payouts.

Income Potential

Speaking of dividend payouts, AMLP offers a pretty substantial one. Last quarter’s payout was $1.01 per quarter. If we round down to $1, the potential payout here is $4 per year. $4 discounted at 6.1% – the 10-year treasury yield plus a 2% risk premium – is worth $65.57. That is 27% upside to today’s price.

Also, AMLP’s dividend has been growing at 6.5% CAGR over the last five years. The previous section, dealing with AMLP’s future prospects, argues that the fund’s portfolio will probably continue doing well going forward. So there should be some growth in the picture. Still, I will leave my “official” price target at $65.57, since that estimate is based on more conservative estimates.

Risks

While AMLP offers the potential for high, growing tax-free income, it does come with certain risks. For non-U.S. investors especially, these risks can be serious. They include:

  • Withholding taxes: Although MLP ETFs remove some of the tax complexity associated with owning individual MLPs, non-U.S. investors nevertheless face at least one extra tax: the U.S. dividend withholding tax. If you are a non-U.S. investor, you have to pay a tax on dividends earned from U.S. stocks. The tax varies by country; Canadians pay 15% to the IRS, for example. This applies to all U.S. stocks, but it is an especially big consideration to high yielding ETFs such as AMLP, as dividends make up a much higher percentage of such funds’ total returns than they do most funds’ total returns.

  • Debt and interest: The pipeline stocks in AMLP’s portfolio generally have high levels of debt and interest. This puts a strain on earnings and in extreme cases puts a company’s solvency at risk.

  • Fee expenses: AMLP charges a 0.85% management fee, which is relatively high by the standards of all ETFs. This fee will likely eat into total returns significantly over the long run.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on AMLP is that it is a high yield fund built on some quality core holdings that have the potential to grow and thrive into the future. It has a high 7.7% yield today, and the yield will likely be even higher in the future. For these reasons, AMLP merits inclusion in a diversified dividend portfolio.

data.title: FYEE's Next Act: Capturing Income Without Surrendering The Upside
data.description:
The Fidelity Yield Enhanced Equity ETF (FYEE) is yet another enhanced income covered call strategy based on an S&P 500-like portfolio. It has an active portfolio that intends to deliver total returns alongside the covered call option layer. The option strategy also appears to be active in terms of positioning and portfolio coverage. Overall performance trends are encouraging enough to consider this lesser-known S&P 500-based income ETF as part of your income portfolio.

Within the covered call income ETFs universe, we usually see two kinds of strategies. Some of these are defensive and aim to maximize income and drawdown mitigation through an aggressive option layer—this at best helps manage volatility and smooths the ownership experience. These also come with the potential to generate income even when markets are flat. The other category of ETFs aims for better total returns by keeping the option layer less aggressive. They do not earn so well in flat markets, and drawdown mitigation is also relatively poorer. But they do allow better upside capture and overall total returns in the long term. This approach funds income more consistently than the aggressive option plays—the assumption is that the drawdown mitigation is limited through covered calls, while the opportunity costs of missing sharp bull rallies are far more. FYEE seems to straddle this positioning actively, according to market conditions, and attempts to bring the best of both worlds together.

Portfolio Differences

FYEE has around 160 holdings that are selected using a methodology that looks at growth and profitability metrics and aims to deliver an alpha over the S&P 500. Since the number of holdings is lower, the first expected repercussion is a slightly more concentrated portfolio. The top 5 holdings in FYEE account for ~27% of the portfolio (compared to ~25.5% in the case of SPY). Currently, the holdings are slightly overweight on all the top mega caps except Apple. The full portfolio I downloaded shows a visibly higher allocation to the AI and semis theme than SPY. It is also underweight vs. SPY on traditional defensive stocks like Coca-Cola, Johnson & Johnson, and the overall utilities cluster.

The second, more relevant repercussion, which is applicable ahead, is a consolidating market, where the AI mega caps digest earnings for a few quarters, and a lack of earnings upgrades and a lack of rerating trigger at the highs keeps share prices broadly flat to slightly positive. This loss of momentum in the growth markets is already visible in the past 3-4 months, and returns ahead could be subdued. In such a scenario, although a more defensive portfolio is not likely to outperform, the risk-reward skew suggests more value in drawdown protection without losing growth alpha. However, the performance gap here is not a lot (given minor differences with SPY at the end of the day)—the important layer that becomes more relevant is how the option strategy is set up for this slower forward regime, I expect.

Option Layer and Performance

The full portfolio shows four weekly covered call positions—51 contracts each at near-the-money strikes within a close range of 6990 to 7070. The notional value of these positions adds up to ~$145m, almost 97% of the net assets held (~$148.5m). That indicates a very aggressive option strategy both in terms of portfolio coverage and strike positioning near the spot levels.

The usual implication of such a strategy is high income in consolidating markets, better drawdown protection (although limited by the premiums gained and usually lower single-digit drawdown differences versus SPY), but a far more restrictive cap on upside gains. Interestingly, this layer is actively managed, and portfolio managers could roll positions as markets move and even change the strike distances and notional coverage. And signs of that active strategy at work are implied from the performance data.

From early 2024 to date, FYEE has closely followed SPYI and SPY itself, which means the upside capturing mechanisms are not sacrificed fully. FYEE's total returns of ~31% are extremely close to SPY's ~35%. And unlike what the current option positioning implies, FYEE follows SPY rallies quite well. Drawdown mitigation is also not as dramatic as an aggressive option layer suggests currently (as seen in the April 2025 sell-off). This points to a dynamic option strategy that becomes more upside-friendly in bull markets. As the bull momentum has slowed down now, FYEE could be positioning itself aggressively with covered calls tactically.

The immediate implication of this strategy is that as markets consolidate or even stay flat, FYEE could generate significant income simply from sold options. If there is a pullback, it could shave off 2-3 percentage points of the fall, i.e., a little more protection than lighter option strategies. The only regime the current setup does not do well in is a sharp rally. I see very low odds of that happening in 2026—2024-25 was when that concern would have weighed heavier on the current option setup. The current setup can even absorb slower growth trajectories, so it is not like it actually caps upside—it simply caps sharp rallies. Therefore, FYEE's agility and current positioning both seem to support a short-term alpha as well as longer-term total returns.

Yields and Taxability

FYEE's yields are also relatively conservative at 7-8%, which preserves NAV and keeps future payouts sizeable. A rule of thumb I use for an SPY-based income fund is whether its yield is in the range of the long-term expectations from the S&P 500. That way, even if the option layer underperforms and the ETF is able to trace the S&P 500 quite closely, its payouts stay well-funded. FYEE meets that payout criteria.

The tax character of the distributions is not clear when I see the 2025 distribution report, but I expect a material presence of ROC or capital gains compared to a more tax-friendly qualified dividend income. Qualified dividends are usually sourced from equity dividends, which are not the major source of payouts from FYEE. Option premium gains are generally taxed as 60% long-term and 40% short-term capital gains. If option premium gains are low in a given period, an ROC component could also be higher than usual. FYEE has a moderately efficient tax structure but remains less so than pure long-term capital gains if investors are in taxable accounts and higher tax brackets. ROC defers tax (by reducing cost basis) but does not eliminate it.

Overall, the tax structure is not a deterrent per se. It suffers from the structural limitations of a covered call setup and does not make FYEE inferior within the peer group. Impacts are moderate overall. What tilts FYEE to a Buy, in my view, is its ability to track SPY and therefore its total returns focus. At the same time, active management means doubling down with a more aggressive option layer in the current phase of market consolidation. That agility in an option layer, a long-term growth tilt in the portfolio, and a stable yield that can stay relevant for long-term investors mean FYEE is a decent candidate within the peer S&P 500 income ETF universe.

data.title: Microsoft: Why I Set A $370 Buy Order
data.description:
MSFT Stock: FQ2 Earnings Met Software Sell-Off

I last wrote on Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) on Jan 9 in an article titled “Microsoft: Super-Investors Are Trimming Their Top Position.” As stated in the title, the article was triggered by the position changes of professional fund managers. The article rated MSFT as Hold.

Since then, a few new developments have emerged around MSFT. In the rest of this article, I will explain why these changes have prompted me to place a $370 limit order to buy MSFT shares. In particular, I will detail three factors behind my limit order: the change of MSFT’s implied volatility (IV) in the option market, its FQ2 2026 earnings report (reported on Jan 28, 2026), and the potential margin pressures in the near future given the impacts of AI on software businesses. I will explain A) why these developments have led me to see a large probability for MSFT share prices to further retreat to the $370 level in the near future (say, within a month), and B) if/when this happens, it’s an excellent entry point for long-term investors.

In a nutshell, the math is quite simple. Any P/E around 20x is a no-brainer for a stock with ROCE (return on capital employed) around 65%, like MSFT in my model. MSFT would be priced around 22x FWD based on its latest EPS projections after the FQ2 ER at a price of $370. At this level of ROCE, a 10% reinvestment rate would provide 6.5% organic real growth rates (and that is before the inflation escalator). And a ~22x P/E would at least provide 4.5% of earnings yield in MSFT’s case, as its owners’ earnings yield consistently exceeds its EPS, leading to a total long-term potential of around 11%.

MSFT Stock: Elevated Implied Volatility And Widened Bollinger Bands

Let me start explaining where the magical number $370 comes from. It came from my modeling of the recent changes in MSFT’s volatilities. Since the release of its FQ2 ER, both the realized volatility (RV, aka historical volatility) and implied volatility (IV) of MSFT have heightened substantially. According to the statistics provided by MarketChameleon shown in the chart below, MSFT’s current IV is 27.6, translating into the 74% percentile rank in the past year. In other words, MSFT’s current IV is roughly among the highest quartile observed in the past. Also, note that the current IV is also below its 20-day RV of 29.0.

Due to the heightened volatility, the Bollinger Bands (BB) of the stock have widened sharply, as shown in the next chart. Before the release of the ER, the low and upper BB for MSFT were about $455 and $505, respectively, resulting in a BB bandwidth of about $50. Now, the low and upper BB have widened to about $390 and $520, respectively, resulting in a BB bandwidth of about $130. Based on the IV and RV data above, my model suggests further widening of the BB in the near term and large odds for its lower band to reach around $370.

Next, I will move on to argue if/when this happens, $370 is a good—i.e., opportunistic - entry point for long-term investors.

MSFT Stock: Why $370 Is A Good Entry Point

The market selloff on MSFT shares (and other software stocks) certainly has good reason, given the potential impact of AI on software developers. But MSFT is not a “normal” software company in my view. It will certainly experience its fair share of pressure given its revenue exposure to its various software segments. As a reflection, I see some margin pressure in the near term.

In FQ2, MSFT reported a normalized EPS of $4.14 per share on total revenue of $81.27 billion (see the snapshot below). The implied net profit margin (NPM) for this period is thus 37.8% on a normalized basis. Looking ahead to the next quarter (with ER release scheduled on April 23), MSFT is expected to earn a normalized EPS of $4.09 on revenue of $81.38 billion, implying an NPM of 37.0%, a contraction of 80 basis points.

Looking ahead, I see such pressure to persist at least during the intermediate term (say the next 2 years or so) due to both the AI impact and also the increase in CAPEX budgets as updated in the ER. However, looking further out, I expect these issues to be temporary and the CAPEX investment to lay the foundation for long-term profitability and growth. I mentioned that MSFT is not a “normal” software company a minute ago, as it is also a key AI player actively developing the very tools that can disrupt software development.

As a reflection of its profitability and growth potential, consensus forecasts point to strong growth potential in the long term, as illustrated in the next chart. For the full fiscal year 2026, the MSFT EPS estimate stands at $16.73, and the EPS is expected to grow to $31.84 in FY 2030, resulting in a 14.2% CAGR for the next 5 fiscal years.

To echo the math mentioned upfront, I don’t expect the higher growth reinvestment rate to remain at its 2025 or 2026 level for the long term. I expect the reinvestment rates to eventually normalize to around 10%, the average of tech-mega caps in the long term. But with an entry P/E of around 20x and a robust ROCE of around 65%, such a normalized reinvestment rate could already offer double-digit annual return potential.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

Besides the above positives and negatives, let me also point out a few notes I’ve gathered about the use of limit buy orders since it is in the title of the article. Based on interactions I’ve had with my readers and other investors, some people love it as a fantastic tool for price precision, while some sneer at it as a market timing attempt. Personal preferences aside, limit orders do come with a distinct set of hidden risks, such as the non-execution risk (the "missed boat" risk) and the opportunity cost of cash. The most valid critic - in my view anyway - on the use of limit orders is shown in the next chart, which illustrates that a $370 limit order and a market order (at the current price of around $400) make little difference in terms of long-term returns (11.0% vs. 10.7% per annum based on my math above). But investors do need to be aware that eventually, we care more about the absolute amount of dollars we profit than annual percentage returns. With the same capital outlay today, a $370 order buys about 8.1% more MSFT shares than a $400 order, a difference (and a sizable one) that will stay as long as we hold the shares, no matter how the annual returns change afterwards.

To close, this MSFT revision focused on 3 new catalysts that emerged since my last analysis: elevated volatility, its FQ2 2026 earnings updates, and the outlook for near-term margin pressure. These factors have created good odds for a $370 entry point in the next month or so, which in turn provides good prospects for double-digit annual total return potential.

data.title: Zscaler Q2 Preview: Cloudflare Is Eating Its Lunch (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

Since the start of the year, the entire cybersecurity landscape has been dragged lower in the SaaSpocalypse meltdown.

As I discussed in my Rubrik post earlier this month, I believe the valuation compression in quality cybersecurity stocks is completely misplaced, as demand for security solutions should only steepen as AI becomes more autonomous, unlike the commoditization risk many SaaS enterprise workflow companies face today. This is because Agentic AI massively expands the attack surface across enterprises and governments, requiring every AI agent-generated action to be inspected, validated, and governed.

So far in this earnings season, we have heard from Cloudflare (NYSE:NET) and Palo Alto Networks (NASDAQ:PANW), which have reported their respective earnings. While Palo Alto has taken a heavy M&A-based approach to spearhead their platformization strategy, both companies reported stronger-than-expected backlog growth during the quarter with further acceleration ahead.

Meanwhile, Zscaler (NASDAQ:ZS), which competes with both Cloudflare and Palo Alto Networks on Network Security has seen a rough start to 2026, with the stock down 25% YTD, as investors fear potential market share losses ahead, especially given that its RPO growth is lagging that of Cloudflare’s.

The company is expected to report its Q2 FY26 earnings on 02/26, where revenue and earnings per share are expected to grow 23.3% and 15%, respectively. While a 200+ basis point beat will help the stock accelerate its revenue growth for two consecutive quarters, all eyes will be on RPO, Net Retention rates, and forward growth guidance.

As an existing investor in the stock, I believe that the management has a tough road ahead to reinstill investor confidence as the competitive landscape intensifies. Meanwhile, I believe there may be a better opportunity instead, which I have been diversifying into since the start of the month.

A Brief Recap of Q1 FY26

When Zscaler reported its Q1 FY26 earnings at the end of November last year, it beat both its revenue and earnings guidance. However, investors were not impressed, with the stock down 28% since the time of my writing.

During the quarter, Zscaler continued to see strong momentum with large customers, where $100K+ and $1M+ ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) customers grew at a CAGR of 18% and 22%, respectively, over the last two years.

When it comes down to its three growth vectors, management expects ARR for AI Security to exceed $0.5B by the end of the fiscal year, while the company has already superseded their target of securing 390 enterprises for Zero Trust Everywhere by the end of FY26, now at a count of 450, and Data Security ARR has also accelerated to $450M as customers are increasingly consolidating on Zscaler’s unified platform.

Their new purchasing program, Z-Flex, is also helping them drive deeper adoption, as it generated $175M in TCV (Total Contract Value), growing 70% QoQ, as customers are significantly expanding their commitment by adding new modules under the Z-Flex program.

However, the stock came under pressure as forward management guidance pointed to no improvement in their full-year non-GAAP operating margin at 22% on a YoY basis, as well as expecting free cash flow margins to slightly decline as they introduce new products at a rapid pace.

Not only that, investors were also not impressed by Zscaler’s RPO, which, despite accelerating for three consecutive quarters to 35%, came under analyst expectations, while its competitor Cloudflare has been growing theirs at a much faster pace and is projected to surpass Zscaler’s revenue by FY29, even though it generated 23% less revenue than Zscaler in FY25. At the same time, Zscaler also faces formidable competition from incumbents like Palo Alto Networks as well in Network Security with their respective Zero Trust/SASE solutions, which we will touch upon in the next section.

Zscaler Has To Put Up A Tough Fight Against Cloudflare and Palo Alto

So far, this earnings season, we have heard from Cloudflare and Palo Alto Networks, where the former is faring much better than the latter, while Zscaler is lagging the group, as can be seen below.

We recently heard from Cloudflare, which crushed its Q4 earnings last week, with revenue growing at an accelerated pace of 34% YoY and New ACV (annual contract value) growing nearly 50% YoY, making it not only a record quarter in absolute ACV dollars but also the fastest growth rate delivered since 2021. What was also impressive was the company reported three consecutive quarters of RPO acceleration culminating at 48%, along with a sequential uptick in Net Retention Rate to 120%.

For the full year FY26, management expects full revenue to grow at roughly the same pace as FY25, between 28 and 29%, but expects margins to contract slightly.

During the earnings call, Cloudflare management firmly asserted Cloudflare’s foundational role in Agentic AI, especially as their low-latency, global edge network is built to facilitate agent-driven traffic, creating a powerful flywheel effect for their Workers platform, which in turn increases demand across Cloudflare’s security and networking stack.

In contrast, Zscaler grew its revenues last quarter by 25.5% and is expected to grow its revenues by 23.31% in Q2 FY26 at the end of this month. Plus, Cloudflare also has a superior Net Retention rate compared to Zscaler’s 115%.

On this note, I will point out, however, that while Zscaler and Cloudflare overlap in the Zero Trust and SASE markets, they differ in their respective network architecture and primary use cases, where the former primarily caters to large enterprises (Forbes 2000) and government, while the latter serves a broader spectrum from individual developers to small businesses and enterprises.

Meanwhile, when it comes to Palo Alto Networks, the company has taken a heavy M&A-based strategy with its acquisition of Chronosphere and CyberArk to spearhead its platformization strategy.

During the quarter, ARR for its NGS (Next Generation Security) platform, consisting of cloud-based security services such as Prisma SASE, Cortex XSIAM, and Strata Firewalls, grew 33% YoY, while its Prisma SASE surpassed $1.5B, growing 40% YoY. What is also important to note is that management significantly raised their FY26 backlog growth projection from 18% to 28%, up 1000 basis points. But this is only made possible because of the revenue boost from its acquisitions.

With Palo Alto management reiterating their $20B in NGS ARR by FY30, there is no doubt that Zscaler faces a tough road, where it has to achieve an accelerating backlog and superior retention rates to gain market share.

There May Be A Better Candidate Instead

Looking forward, Zscaler is set to report its Q2 FY26 earnings at the end of this month, where it is projected to generate revenue growth of 23.31% YoY for the quarter. Should it beat consensus estimates by over 200 basis points, it would mark an acceleration for two quarters in a row.

However, all eyes will be on RPO growth, Net Retention Rate and forward backlog and revenue guidance at this point, which will determine the trajectory of the stock. With both Cloudflare and Palo Alto Networks significantly raising their forward backlog projections, analysts will expect Zscaler to deliver similar results, with no slippage in Net Retention rates.

The stock is currently trading at a forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 45, with a projected 16.55% YoY growth in earnings per share to $3.82. However, when we take FY28 earnings per share of $5.50 into account, the forward PE ratio compresses down to 30. Since the company is expected to grow its earnings in the high-teens range over the next two to three years, I believe it deserves at least a 2x premium over the 10-year average S&P 500 price-to-earnings multiple.

This would translate to a P/E ratio of 38-44, or a price target of $242 per share, representing an upside of 44% from current levels.

While this is an attractive risk-reward for new investors who want to initiate a position in the company, I believe that there is a better candidate at the moment for a long-term investment opportunity: Cloudflare. While Cloudflare is significantly more expensive than Zscaler, with a forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 169, the company is growing at a faster rate and is expected to remain that way over the next three to five years.

At this point, investors are more patient towards high-multiple companies that are growing much faster than expectations versus the ones that are at risk of losing market share. I am not saying that Zscaler is there yet, but investors fear the possible outcome, which means that the company has to significantly outperform to win back investor confidence.

My Final Verdict and Conclusions

I am not giving up on Zscaler yet, but I have been diversifying into Cloudflare and CrowdStrike as well. At the moment, Zscaler management has to demonstrate far better RPO growth and forward guidance with a stable Net Retention Rate in order to reinstill investor confidence. Failure to do so will further confirm investor fears of potential market share losses, which won’t be taken lightly.

As a result, I will downgrade the stock to a “hold” at current levels as we wait for some more direction in the Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Itron: Acquisitions Come At A Price
data.description:
Back in November I concluded that Itron, Inc. (ITRI) became acquisitive amidst a mixed operating performance. In theory, Itron enjoys a sound business position, yet it saw muted sales growth and soft bookings, which prevented me from seeing appeal despite very modest earnings multiples being applied.

The company announced two substantial acquisitions in the fall, which came at a substantial price tag, making me a bit cautious as few details on their contribution were disclosed. By now these appear to be rather costly acquisitions with relatively limited near-term financial contribution, making me rather cautious here.

Industrial IoT

Itron is an industrial IoT business as well as an edge intelligence leader that provides innovative ways for utilities and cities to manage both their energy and water assets.

The company provides communicating endpoints and distributed intelligence-enabled solutions. The company has more than 300 million endpoints across these infrastructure networks, including nearly 17 million intelligence-enabled endpoints. The company employs about 5,000 workers, who provide such intelligence solutions to nearly 8,000 customers.

Some 80% of sales are generated in North America, complemented by smaller activities in Europe and the Asia-Pacific region. About two-thirds of revenue are classified as networked solutions, complemented by device solutions and outcome segments.

The company benefits from many long-term demand drivers providing increased demand for its services. This includes more strain on infrastructure, driven by aging infrastructure, greater employment of BES systems, and electrical vehicles plugged into the network. Other trends include more extreme weather, with all such trends making that demand and supply tend to vary much more, requiring intelligent grid solutions, like the ones provided by the business here.

Despite this sound positioning, the business has demonstrated a relatively modest operating performance. After all, over the past decade, the company has grown sales by almost 20% to $2.4 billion, with sales growth trailing inflation substantially over this period of time. In all fairness, historically, operating margins have come in between single digits and the ten percent mark, these now having risen to the low teens.

Such mixed performance has been demonstrated in the share price as well, which has seen real ups and downs. A $100 stock in 2008 was down to the $30 mark in the mid-2010s. Shares rose to the $100 mark in 2021, fell to $50 in 2023, and then momentum pushed shares up to a high of around $140 last summer, with current trading just under the $100 mark.

About The Expectations

Following a strong 2023, the company grew 2024 sales by 12% to $2.44 billion. GAAP operating profits of $264 million translated into operating margins of 11%, these margins having been depressed by amortization and restructuring charges. All this translated into adjusted earnings of around $5.50 per share.

So despite this sound positioning, the company guided for a pretty mediocre 2025. Sales were seen as flattish between $2.40 and $2.50 billion, with adjusted earnings seen around $5.40 per share, all this being pretty flat.

As the year progressed, divergent operational performance was clearly seen, with sales trending soft while earnings came in stronger. The company had cut the full-year sales guidance by nearly a hundred million to $2.35-$2.36 billion as of the third quarter. Adjusted earnings were actually seen at $6.89 per share, up nearly a dollar and fifty cents compared to the start of the year.

Trading near the $100 mark and factoring in a tiny net cash position, valuations were not too demanding, with earnings seen near $7 per share in 2025, driven by a strong margin performance. The question was how sustainable all that was, given the softness on the topline and softer booking numbers. Valued at $4.7 billion, two substantial acquisitions were announced: a $325 million purchase of Urbint and a $525 million acquisition of Locusview.

Combined, costing $850 million, the two deals came in at 20% of the prevailing enterprise valuation, with few details announced, making it hard to form an opinion on these, as I did not like the limited disclosure.

Mixed Signs

News about the business arrived in February, with fourth-quarter sales reported down 7% to $572 million, as the business ended the year on a soft note. This was attributed to portfolio optimization efforts and unfortunate timing of orders, but investors have heard such explanations all before.

So far, so good, with full-year sales down 3% to $2.37 billion. Fortunately, the margin picture has been much stronger, with adjusted earnings of $7.13 per share coming in a dollar and fifty cents ahead of last year. Non-GAAP earnings of $330 million look pretty impressive, driven by strong margin gains, as the reconciliation to GAAP earnings is pretty clean. Net debt was reported at $227 million thanks to the acquisition of Urbint closing during the quarter, while the purchase of Locusview closed in January, just after the year ended.

The Urbint deal closed on Nov. 3, with these solutions generating $3.0 million in sales in the quarter. Based on a near two-month contribution, that suggests a run rate close to $20 million in annual sales, suggesting that the purchase came at a price.

More will become clear as these two deals will become part of the so-called "Resiliency Solutions" segment, a newly established business here. On the conference call, management indicated that this segment will see $65-$70 million in sales this year, which, based on the Locusview deal closing very early in the year, is representative of the combined financial contribution of these two firms.

This implies a total of 12-13 times sales has been paid for these two deals, a massive discrepancy from the roughly 2 times sales multiple at which the own business was valued.

A Touch Light

Much attention for me is on the outlook for 2026, and to be frank, it's rather disappointing. Full-year sales are seen as pretty flat, around a midpoint of $2.40 billion, plus or minus $50 million, which suggests no real organic growth. Moreover, some margin compression is seen, with adjusted earnings seen around $6.00 per share, plus or minus 25 cents. This is down well over a dollar per share compared to 2025, in part driven by the two recent deals, expected to have a $0.38 per share detrimental impact on earnings this year.

After 2025 was somewhat of a strange year, with sales trending soft but earnings being strong, some normalization in those margins is seen in 2026, which is not too surprising. However, there's the element of expensive dealmaking activity, which comes with near-term dilution (expected to have a neutral impact on 2027 earnings) as well, as the dealmaking hunt has been expensive, although manageable.

On the other hand, the company can pay this and can use a real organic growth driver here, after the operating margins of its standalone business have been rather soft.

Trading down to $95, the earnings estimates for the coming year are seen around 16 times, which still looks pretty low. Of course there's some debt employed here as well, but it's still manageable, as the company should be able to benefit from growth in the innovative solutions business. This new resiliency business makes up nearly 3% of pro forma sales, too small to move the needle, but potentially providing some sort of organic growth driver here.

In between the many mixed messages ranging from softer bookings and sales to stronger margins, these margins are in a partial reverse in 2026. While some future-proof M&A moves are made, these come at an expense, at 20% of the prevailing valuation, while pro forma sales will jump just by about 3%. However, these deals bring expertise and likely cross-selling opportunities, all designed to improve the pace of organic growth, which has been rather uninspiring lately. This is also the reason shares have been lagging peers such as Badger Meter (BMI) and Xylem (XYL) and some other (also more widely diversified) peers, which trade at much more demanding multiples.

Weighing all this together, I'm still not getting comfortable enough to build confidence here. Before initiating a position, I would like to see a non-demanding multiple come down further. Only in the $80s, I regard the additional earnings yield as sufficient to be compensated for lackluster organic growth, or in case the company really starts to deliver on accelerated organic growth itself.

data.title: Financial Institutions, Inc.: Back On Track After A Challenging Year
data.description:
Right around this time in 2025, Financial Institutions, Inc. (FISI) was reeling from the impact of a disappointing Q4 2024 financial report. Included in the results from the final three months of that year was the impact of a restructuring plan that delivered a significant blow to the company’s profitability. The first step of the plan was a public offering of stock to raise roughly $95 million, which was no small event for a company that had a market cap around $400 million at the end of 2024.

The second part of the company’s restructuring efforts included selling just over $650 million in available-for-sale securities, resulting in a pre-tax loss of $100 million. This action led to FISI reporting losses for both the quarter and the full year. Financial Institutions lost about 15% of its value between mid-February and the start of April in 2025, but the short-term pains from the company appear to have set it up for potential long-term gains.

Fast forward to the end of last year, and FISI appears to have put most of the negative sentiment from the past behind it. On January 29, the company released its Q4 2025 financial results, which included its fourth consecutive earnings beat and a positive outlook for the year ahead. Based on the current data available, I think that FISI deserves a cautious Buy rating with potential for additional gains above the market average this year.

Company Overview

Financial Institutions is a holding company that controls a banking subsidiary, Five Star Bank, and a wealth management division that operates under the name Courier Capital, LLC. Five Star Bank has roughly 50 total branch locations, all of which are located in Central and Western New York. Courier Capital serves clients in five offices – three in New York, one in Pittsburgh, PA, and one in Sarasota, FL.

As of February 19, FISI has a market cap of $669.3 million, which shows the gains the company has already experienced over the last 14 months. At the end of last year, the company controlled $6.27 billion in assets. The bulk of that total was divided between a securities portfolio worth $1.00 billion and a loan portfolio valued at $4.66 billion. A breakdown of the loan mix from Five Star Bank can be seen in the graph below.

Commercial loans make up about two-thirds of the total loans from the bank, with commercial real estate being the largest segment in that group. Non-owner-occupied CRE represented 20% of the total portfolio. Owner-occupied CRE adds another 7%.

Consumer indirect auto loans make up a significant part of the total loan mix for the bank at 17%. This is a share that has been decreasing over the last three years. In March of 2025, Financial Institutions did settle a lawsuit related to its auto lending practices that cost the company roughly $23 million.

The weighted average FICO score of a Five Star Bank borrower in this indirect lending category is 718, which helps reduce some of the risk for these loans. Auto loan delinquencies nationwide hit a 15-year high two months ago, and Senator Elizabeth Warren called for an investigation into rising car repossession rates at the start of this month. Frankly, this is a loan type that I would want to avoid as an investor, but FISI has been able to manage these loans with a net charge-off ratio that was lower at the end of 2025 than it was at the end of the previous year.

FISI currently has an attractive dividend yield of 3.68%. On February 13, the company announced an increase of $0.01 from its prior quarterly payout of $0.31. From 2011 until 2023, FISI was able to string together 13 straight years of higher annual payouts, a streak that ended in 2024.

Analysis of Financial Institutions, Inc.

Normally when I discuss quarterly and end-of-year results for a company, I would compare them to the corresponding time periods from the previous year. However, since Q4 2024 contained such a significant negative event, the comparisons are not as meaningful. There were also other “one-time” events during the year, like the sale of insurance assets in Q2 2024, that make annual comparisons a bit more uneven.

Even without this kind of comparison, diluted earnings of $0.96 per share in Q4 and $3.61 for FY 2025 look impressive on a standalone basis for a financial stock trading at $33.40 per share. Return on assets reached 1.27% in Q4, and return on equity came in at 12.53%, with both numbers being about 70 basis points higher than at the end of the first quarter of last year. Net interest margin stood at 3.62% in Q4 2025 after being just 2.91% in Q4 of the previous year.

The company made significant improvements in shareholders’ equity in 2025, rising from $551.7 million to $611.6 million in a 12-month period. That increase, along with a slight reduction in the overall shares outstanding from the company, has helped give the stock’s tangible book value per share a double-digit boost over the last year. After ending 2024 at $24.45, TBV for FISI rose to $27.84 at the end of 2025, a 13.9% improvement. At its current price, FISI now trades at a reasonable price-to-TBV multiple of 1.20.

One additional positive characteristic of Financial Institutions is its high share of non-interest income relative to its total income. In Q4 2025, the company recorded $11.9 million in non-interest income, which represented 18.6% of the $64.1 million in total revenues for the company. Banking services fees represented the largest share of non-interest income at $4.6 million, a total that was 29% higher than in Q4 2024. Investment advisory income amounted to $3.1 million in Q4 2025, up 20% from the linked quarter from the previous year.

Risks to Consider

The loan portfolio that is controlled by Five Star Bank carries notable risk for the company, even as its quality metrics are in a reasonable range. The total exposure to CRE at the end of 2025, as reported by the company, has an allocation of 19% to office properties, which have been under significant pressure nationwide over the last several years. However, there are some signs that this sector has reached an "intermediate phase" that could relieve at least some of the stress for lenders.

Asset quality ratios for FISI at the end of 2025 were slightly higher than I like to see in a small regional bank, but the trends for these numbers had them moving in the right direction. When compared to the end of 2024, the company’s non-performing loan ratio lost 15 basis points. Non-performing assets dropped 11. Net charge-offs fell from 0.25% to 0.21%. These results and the trends associated with them can be seen below.

Five Star Bank, like all regional banks, would face additional challenges in the face of a meaningful pullback in the US economy. Slower economic growth would lead to slower loan growth and perhaps increased defaults on current loans. Financial Institutions would face added headwinds from a correction in the stock market, as they do rely on income generated by its wealth management division, Currier Capital. Declines in assets under management would reduce the fees that its operations are able to generate. Of the non-interest income that the company reported in Q4 2025, 26.1% was collected by Currier Capital.

The limited geographic diversity also adds risk to any investment here. New York is ranked 50th, right behind California and New Jersey, by the Tax Foundation for its tax competitiveness. The state scored poorly because of its high individual tax rates, high sales taxes, and high property taxes. At a time when more and more businesses and high-net-worth individuals seem willing to move to find a more reasonable tax burden, these numbers may matter more than ever. The governor of Louisiana openly campaigned for residents of New York City to move to his state after the recent results of New York City’s mayoral election were finalized.

Final Thoughts

As part of its Q4 2025 earnings report, the company gave guidance for the year ahead. A complete list of the expectations for 2026 is shown below, but the important points worth noting for me were the modest increase in loans and improved financial metrics like ROA and NIM. Deposit growth is expected to be slightly slower than the bank’s loan growth, and net interest income may moderate slightly in the year ahead. However, all totaled, the company’s outlook seems to put it on track for earnings of $3.92 per share, as analysts expect. This would give the stock a sub-nine PE ratio to go with the previously mentioned reasonable price-to-TBV ratio.

There are certainly a few things I do not like about the bank’s operations, with the auto lending portfolio being at the top of that list, but FISI seems to have been able to manage this risk well over an extended period of time. The geographic footprint of the bank could make it an attractive takeover candidate for a larger bank that is looking for increased presence in New York as M&A activity continues to benefit from more relaxed regulation from the current presidential administration.

Financial Institutions and its banking operations are not the best that I have seen in recent months, but the company certainly seems to be back on track after a choppy end to 2024. I think the stock is worth a cautious Buy at its current market price of $33.40.

data.title: Amazon: History Rhymes As E-Commerce Playbook Meets AI
data.description:
Investors looking to take advantage of the volatility in tech stocks might find Amazon (AMZN) attractive. The e-commerce and cloud juggernaut has achieved dominant positioning at a global scale. The company is generating stunning e-commerce margins despite offering best-in-class shipping speeds. I expect AI and robotics to drive growth and margins higher long term. AWS is posting accelerated growth, which is backed by management’s decision to invest even more aggressively. I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating on the stock.

AMZN Stock Price

I last covered AMZN in December, where I upgraded the stock due to the potential “catch-up trade.” That has not worked out so far, with the stock falling double digits since.

AMZN trades with great volatility despite having two of the most durable businesses in the world.

AMZN Stock Key Metrics

At times it can be humorous to see the market oscillate between love and hate for the company’s e-commerce and cloud divisions. Currently, the market appears to be cooling on the company’s cloud prospects.

In the most recent quarter, AMZN generated 14% YoY revenue growth to $213.4 billion, exceeding guidance of between $206 billion and $213 billion.

The company generated 18% YoY growth in operating income to $24.98 billion, coming in at the high end of guidance of between $21 billion and $26 billion. I suspect that investors were disappointed on this front, as the company has historically delivered beats on the bottom line. At this stage in the game, the top-line beat matters less than the bottom-line disappointment.

The company delivered solid results in North American e-commerce, with sales jumping 10% YoY and operating income rising 24% YoY. This segment delivered a stunning 9% operating margin in the quarter and a 6.9% margin over the trailing twelve months. It is incredible that the company can deliver faster than competitors yet still generate strong profit margins.

The company’s international e-commerce saw 17% YoY revenue growth, but operating income declined 21%. That said, on the conference call, management noted that it recorded a $1.1 billion charge for “resolution of tax disputes associated with our stores business in Italy and the settlement of a lawsuit.”

Amazon Web Services (‘AWS’), the company’s cloud computing arm, saw top-line growth accelerate to 24%, with operating income jumping 17%. The company is no longer looking like a laggard in the AI race.

The company has, however, seen free cash flow continue to trend lower. Like the other hyperscalers, AMZN has been investing heavily in building out its data center footprint.

AMZN ended the quarter with $123 billion of cash and marketable securities versus $65.6 billion of debt. The strong balance sheet eases my concerns regarding the ambitious investment plans, which include $200 billion of planned CapEx in 2026. Looking ahead, management guided the company to see up to 15% YoY revenue growth in the first quarter.

Management discussed their CapEx plans, which were the highest among mega-cap tech, as being due to their desire to “invest aggressively here to be the leaders.” I suspect that Wall Street may be viewing the elevated CapEx as instead indicating that the company is needing to play “catch up.” Yet these fears aren’t showing in the results. Management noted that their chips business is now “over $10 billion in annual revenue run rate, growing triple-digit percentages year-over-year.” Management noted that they are seeing customers “completely reinventing their applications, agentic coding applications, legal applications.” Management has confidence “that these investments will yield strong returns on invested capital,” and I believe them. Their track record in e-commerce speaks for itself, and I am honestly surprised that the market is choosing to penalize AMZN and others for aggressive CapEx spending even as we are already seeing tangible results in cloud growth.

Speaking of e-commerce, management noted that this segment has been a beneficiary of AI. They noted that “customers who use Rufus are about 60% more likely to complete a purchase.” Anecdotally, I have found Rufus to be quite helpful. Management noted that “Everyday Essentials grew nearly twice as fast as all other categories in the U.S., representing one out of every three units sold.” I view this to be an important data point, as it is clear evidence that AMZN is making progress towards eating all of retail.

I have noticed (also anecdotally) that much of our grocery spending is now going through Amazon, which is something that I would have never imagined just a few years ago. The company appears to be reaping the fruits of their intensive investment in e-commerce infrastructure, just in time for management to turn their sights on investing aggressively in AI.

Is AMZN Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

AMZN stock saw some strength on Friday following news that the Supreme Court had ruled against the tariffs. I am doubtful that this is really the end of tariffs, as the Trump administration has other avenues to maintain their tariff policies and has already announced more tariffs. Even so, AMZN stock looks too cheap here. The stock was recently trading at just around 27x forward earnings.

Consensus estimates call for the company to sustain low-double-digit top-line growth moving forward. I find these estimates highly achievable given the resilient e-commerce growth coupled with the accelerating AWS growth.

I have previously assumed 9% long-term margins for the e-commerce business. Given the company’s rapidly improving margins in North America, I can see a clear argument that this target is too conservative. If we continue using that target while projecting a 30x earnings multiple, then we can value the e-commerce business at $1.75 trillion (assuming 10% growth in 2026). That 30x earnings multiple looks reasonable considering that Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST) trade at 42x and 49x earnings, respectively. Of course, one could make the argument that WMT and COST stocks look richly valued, but I expect AMZN to take market share from these two names over the long term as they struggle to compete on both price and speed.

Over the long term, I expect robotics and AI to drive margins higher at AMZN e-commerce. Together with the company’s logistics dominance, I expect operating leverage to be the main catalyst to drive value discovery there.

With the market cap standing at around $2.24 trillion, AWS is at an implied $490 billion valuation. Yet this segment is operating at a $142 billion annual revenue run rate, and thus I view the current stock valuation as being undervalued. Assuming this segment sees 25% growth in 2026, we might arrive at around $160 billion in 2026 revenues. I can see this segment generating at least 35% net margins. I view that as conservative given that it is already generating 35.4% operating margins. Assuming a 30x earnings multiple, this segment is worth around $1.68 trillion. Adding these together, we arrive at $3.43 trillion in value, or $320 per share. I see visible catalysts in 2026, as I expect the company to continue showing accelerating AWS growth as it brings capacity online. I expect strong numbers (and resilient margins) to eventually swing sentiment to a more positive view of the ambitious CapEx spending.

AMZN Stock Risks

It is possible that the elevated CapEx is here to stay. Perhaps AMZN needs to maintain massive spending just to keep up with hyperscaler competitors. Perhaps AMZN cannot produce a competitive model, or perhaps customers end up not being OK with “good enough.” If the economy swings into a recession, AMZN may see pressure on its e-commerce volumes. AI might be a bubble, and the bursting of this bubble may lead to pressure on AWS revenues.

AMZN Stock Conclusion

I view AMZN as being one of the biggest winners of AI. Besides offering AI computing services through AWS, the e-commerce arm is likely to have low-hanging fruit for the taking. Wall Street appears hesitant on the company’s CapEx forecast, yet this is the very company that has shown an ability to drive strong returns from elevated investment. AMZN is a core holding in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I reiterate my "Strong Buy" rating on the stock.

data.title: Gentherm Is Finally Worthy Of An Upgrade As Its Transformation Progresses
data.description:
February 19th was an interesting day for shareholders of Gentherm Incorporated (THRM). The stock closed up 1.3% after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. That's not a huge move. But it is a little surprising. While revenue came in above what analysts were hoping for, earnings per share and adjusted earnings per share both fell short of expectations. Normally when profitability falls short of what analysts hope for, the end result is a decline in share price. But this time is different.

A lot of that, I think, stems from the fact that management came out with what I would consider to be solid guidance for this year and next. Looking at the data provided, I do see an interesting company. Even if the firm does not hit expectations for the next couple of years, it is still trading at attractive levels. Naturally, this makes me bullish about the business.

This might be surprising considering my recent history with the stock. Back in November of last year, I made the decision to reaffirm the company as a Hold candidate. At that time, I was worried about contracting profitability and short-term economic conditions. To be honest with you, the economic picture still concerns me. But considering the recent performance that management achieved, as well as the company's valuation, I believe that upgrading it to a soft Buy makes sense here.

Time For An Upgrade

For the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, Gentherm reported revenue of $382.8 million. That represents an increase of 8.5% over the $352.9 million the company reported a year earlier. This is a pretty impressive increase. Some of it, however, ended up being caused by foreign currency fluctuations. Without that, the revenue increase would have been a more modest 5.6%. That is still attractive in and of itself, though.

Based on the data provided, the company saw growth in some really important areas. Most significant was its climate and comfort electronics category. Revenue shot up 81.7% from $4.1 million to $7.4 million. Management continues to invest in this market. Specifically, this refers to the furniture brand category that I have talked about in the past. This still accounts for a very small piece of the overall pie. But even so, any sort of progress on this front is great to see.

For context, these particular types of products include certain software that is used to determine the position of a power seat and that can also control the memory seat module. The company uses a sensor that's integrated into the steering wheel so they can monitor whether a driver's hands are maintaining contact with the steering wheel.

Climate control interiors, climate control seats, and lumbar and massage comfort solutions all experienced nice increases in revenue year over year. It is worth noting that, during their earnings call, management said that they locked down another contract with a leading global furniture brand in order to supply these products. They even described their momentum in the market as accelerating. And with the business only first beginning discussions with potential customers in the middle of 2025 and manufacturing and delivering components starting up in January of this year, it's clear that management is serious about this opportunity.

Revenue for the company did end up exceeding expectations to the tune of $10.9 million. I wish I could say the same thing happened when it came to profitability. But that would be a lie. Earnings per share actually contracted sharply from $0.49 to $0.10, pushing net income down from $15.3 million to only $3 million. The earnings per share here did fall short of expectations by $0.46. The good news was that adjusted earnings per share did perform better. It jumped from $0.29 to $0.49 year over year. But even though that was better than it was in 2024, it still fell short of expectations by $0.07. Overall, that still didn't mean a rise in adjusted net profits from $9.1 million to $15.2 million.

Unfortunately, all other profitability metrics did worsen. Operating cash flow dipped from $36.5 million to $29 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $45.8 million to $14.8 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business fell slightly from $41.4 million to $40.6 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2025 fiscal year in its entirety compared to 2024. In this case, revenue increased. But the picture when it comes to profits and cash flows was most certainly more of a mixed bag. Honestly, considering market conditions, I am not surprised here. Although management has not quantified it recently, tariffs are most certainly an issue here. But we also have the fact that this is a difficult space to play in already. Profitability can be volatile from one year to the next, especially as the automotive market changes. This is where some risk for the company certainly lies.

Last year, when it comes to the U.S., for instance, new vehicle retail sales amounted to 16.3 million. That number this year should be around 15.8 million. Management, citing S&P Global, said that light vehicle production in the key markets that the company plays in will be about 1% lower year over year. But even with this, and even with the company exiting certain businesses, management believes that innovations will allow it to continue to capture a growing share of this market.

Even as market conditions deteriorate because of trade war issues and soft consumer demand, management expects revenue for 2026 to be between $1.5 billion and $1.6 billion. Even the low end of that is just a bit above what the company saw in 2025. From that, the firm anticipates EBITDA of between $175 million and $195 million. At the midpoint, this would likely mean adjusted net profits of $74.2 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $112.4 million.

When it comes to 2027, management has offered some thoughts as well. Unfortunately, we don't know what to expect when it comes to profitability beyond this year. But thanks to additional investments the company is making, including between $45 million and $55 million for this year alone, the business believes that revenue in 2027 will amount to roughly $1.7 billion.

If we use the estimates provided for 2026 and historical results for 2025, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced at the moment. To be honest with you, these are very low multiples, especially those that look at the picture through the lens of the 2026 fiscal year. And relative to other similar enterprises, the stock looks cheap also. In that same chart, you can see that two of the five companies that I stacked it up against ended up being cheaper than it is on a price-to-operating cash flow basis. But when it comes to the other two valuation approaches, our candidate ended up being the cheapest of the group.

One thing that should not be ignored is that, in addition to focusing on organic growth by means of innovation and other initiatives, the company is also working to grow in ways that are not organic. On January 29th, for instance, the company announced an agreement that will see the business merge with the Performance Technologies business of Modine Manufacturing Company (MOD). This transaction is interesting because it will expand Gentherm’s focus in the thermal management solutions space.

In addition to giving the company immediate scale, it will also open up the door to some cost savings. Management expects, for instance, for around $25 million of annualized cost savings to be captured. This will come through cross-selling initiatives, product integration, and the entrance into new global markets. This will result in about $147 million worth of EBITDA from revenue of about $1.11 billion. That essentially pushes Gentherm up to a $2.6 billion firm.

According to the terms of the agreement, Gentherm is paying $1 billion for these assets. This will take the form of $210 million of debt and $790 million worth of stock. The stock component of the deal will consist of the issuance of 21 million shares. However, Gentherm has dropped since then, reducing the effective price for the shares being traded to $658 million. That gives us an implied total price today of $868 million.

I already covered a detailed analysis of how Gentherm is valued right now. But it would be helpful to compare the company to this newly acquired enterprise, especially considering how significant the transaction is. After all, the enterprise value of Gentherm today is only $971.8 million. So this truly is a transformative maneuver by the company. Because we are limited in terms of what metrics we have access to, the best way to view this is through the lens of the enterprise value in relation to revenue and enterprise value in relation to EBITDA.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how the stock is valued on an enterprise value-to-revenue basis compared to these assets that are being acquired. Clearly, Gentherm is paying a premium relative to what the company is currently valued at. In the chart below, I then looked at the picture through the lens of the enterprise value in relation to EBITDA. And once again, we can see that Gentherm is indeed paying a premium for these assets. This is even with the assumption that synergies come to fruition. The good news is that while this certainly does weigh on the company to some extent, even these soon-to-be-acquired assets are looking rather attractive from a valuation standpoint.

If we focus on the longer investment horizon, however, even paying a premium for these assets can make sense. Thanks in large part to the aforementioned acquisition, management expects that by the year 2030, the business should be able to generate around $3.5 billion in revenue. From that, they anticipate EBITDA of $525 million. This would likely translate, when we factor in the additional debt load and interest corresponding to it, to adjusted net profits at that time of $200.5 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $309 million.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart below the trading multiples of the company projected out to the year 2030. In the event that the business is still trading at that time at the multiples that it's at today, we would end up with annualized upside of between 11.4% and 11.8% between now and then. But when you consider that the stock is cheaper than other similar firms and that management expects overall profit margins to be higher than they are today, which would theoretically justify higher trading multiples, I wouldn't be surprised if this prospect is capable of upside of between 12% and 14% annually if things go well. Considering that the S&P 500 typically averages between 10% and 11% a year, I consider this a win.

Takeaway

Based on the data available right now, it is my assertion that while certain issues remain, mostly centered around weakening economic conditions, Gentherm makes for a better prospect today than it did when I last wrote about the company. The stock is attractively priced, and management continues to grow the business nicely. Unfortunately, we have seen some mixed results on the bottom line. But in the grand scheme of things, that's neither surprising nor a deal breaker. Because of this, I think that upgrading THRM stock to a Buy is the right move here.
